title: Building a First Budget
summary: Turn income, needs, and wants into an actual plan.
doThisToday: Write down your real monthly income and your real needs total. Just those two numbers.

cards.prompt: You know needs vs. wants and the 50/30/20 split. Time to build a real budget.
cards.explanation: It's just three steps: total your income, cover your needs, then split what's left.
This is every dollar coming in during the month — paycheck, allowance, birthday money you're not immediately spending.

cards.prompt: You earn $150/week from your job (4 weeks this month) plus $40 in allowance. What's your total monthly income?
cards.explanation: $150 × 4 = $600, plus $40 allowance = $640 total income for the month.

cards.prompt: Step 2: List your needs and give each one a dollar amount before planning anything else.
cards.explanation: Needs come first because they're non-negotiable — you're not choosing whether to pay them, just how much.

cards.prompt: Which of these belongs on your 'needs' list?
options: Bus pass to get to school
New sneakers because yours are 'kind of old'
cards.explanation: Getting to school reliably is a need. The rest are wants, even if some feel more urgent than others.

cards.prompt: Your needs this month: $60 phone bill + $45 gas + $25 for your share of a family streaming/utility bill. What's the total?

cards.explanation: What's left is your 'free money' — split it between wants and savings using a ratio like 50/30/20, adjusted to your real needs.

cards.prompt: Income $640 minus needs $130. How much is left for wants + savings combined?
cards.explanation: $640 − $130 = $510 left to split between wants and savings — you decide the exact ratio.

cards.prompt: True or false: a budget only works if you never spend on wants.
cards.explanation: A budget isn't about cutting out fun — it's about deciding on purpose how much fun you can afford, so you're not surprised when your account hits $12 the week before payday.

cards.prompt: There's a stricter version of budgeting some adults swear by.
cards.frontText: What's it called when every single dollar of your income gets assigned a job — spending, saving, or debt — until there's nothing left unassigned?
cards.backText: Zero-based budgeting. Income minus every planned expense and savings goal should equal zero — not because you spent it all, but because every dollar has a job, even the dollars going to savings.
cards.explanation: It's more detailed than 50/30/20, but the core idea is the same one you just practiced: give every dollar a purpose before it disappears into "where did that go?"

cards.prompt: Recap: put the 3 budgeting steps back in order.

steps.label: Split what's left between wants and savings

cards.explanation: Income first because you can't plan around a number you don't know. Needs second because they're non-negotiable. Everything else is what's actually left to decide on.

cards.prompt: That's the whole system: income, then needs, then split what's left.
cards.explanation: Do this every time money comes in and you'll always know exactly where you stand.
